tip_text: Circle the footbag with your LEG, between toe kicks. try moving your leg at the same speed, as the footbag ascends, so must your leg at the same speed -- similar to descention. [[dont circle TOO wide : think JUST up, then down -- the climax should "feel" like a regular toe kick]]
Just bend your support leg at the knees just a bit then try practicing going out then in circling the footbag at the same rate as it fall it will look cool and will amaze your freinds.
Don't waste your time with this move. It's much harder than it should be. Instead, work on sole kick or around the world delay.

Steve's right. This move won't improve any skills
you will need for any other trick. It won't even
improve your dexterities! The only thing it may
improve, is the precision of your toe kicks. But,
you should already have those dialed by now
anyway!
It doesn't hurt to try these when you're bored or
discouraged, though. So the magic formula is:
have great ATW stalls! Seriously! The around the
world stall is easier to learn. Try this move
when your skills have plateaued and you need to do
something zany, old school, and completely
different, or when you don't want to show off at
all in your circle of kickers. Everyone needs
something new to do when they feel they've hit a
rut.
As with a lot of dex tricks, balance is the thing that will help you pull it off. Don't be fooled, this trick isn't a particularly fast one, timing is essential.
The key is to be relaxed and not to place to much emphasis on this trick, it's and easy trick to accomplish. Once you've learned this trick then learn the stalls which will help you in the long run.

This trick isn't particularly difficult, and it is a neat looking supplemental move. Why not add it to your arsenal? looks slick with a slap juggle finish. spend an hour or two on it and then move on to more glorious tricks.
I agree I found this trick quite simple to learn all you need to do is relax and work at it for a day or two
Rember to lift the knee insted of circling the whole calf laterally

These guys have the right idea. Around The World is a relatively simple trick that just takes practice in order to get consistent. The key is to be relaxed, and try to focus on executing it on one smooth and controlled motion. After stalling the footbag on the toe, try and keep your foot as straight as possible when you kick it up. Also try and stay close to the footbag when you're bringing your leg around. This will make it much easier to get your leg around in time.

The most important thing for being able to hit
repetitive, controlled clipper kicks is to bend
your support leg deeply. If you have to jump
into it to make the support leg bend, so be it.
When you get the support leg bent, (I'm talking 90
degrees.) You will have a flat surface on the
inner sole. Bend your support leg.
*Also...keep your shoulders open to the hack.*

The clipper kick will develop certain skills
required for the clipper stall: one of the most
important freestyle moves. The clipper kick is
also really fun and styley, so here we go...
The balance required for this move is VERY
important! Try this: WITHOUT A FOOTBAG- stand on
one leg. Bend this leg between 45-90 degrees.
Put all of your weight on the balls of your foot,
NOT the heel! With your other foot, flex your
ankle inward like an inside kick or stall. Wrap
your foot around to the cross-body position.
Bend forward a little and turn your upper body
just a bit to look straight down at the instep of
your kicking foot; your eyes must be directly
above your instep. Move your kicking foot up and
down a litle bit. Feel all those weird muscles
cramping and straining? Those are the muscles
you must train to do the clipper kick! This is
the ideal position to land in while doing a
clipper kick. Now anyone can just fling their
foot back and try to kick a bag, but it will just
fly off in a random direction. The above
position, though, will produce controlled,
straight, consistent kicks.
Now try it! Just set the footbag to yourself
with a good kick, or with your hand, and
immediately step quickly into the position. You
should step off of the foot you intend to clipper
kick with and plant your support leg, and end in
the clipper position. Over time, this will become
more and more fluid and natural, just like any
other kick! Practice!

To learn the position, bend your support leg to a 90 degree angle, bend your other leg into a 90 degree angle and curve the other leg into the cross body position and stay there for a minute or 2, make the muscles burn. This is the way I learned to do this kick

This is just like doing a regular kick to me. all you need to do is turn your body about 45 degrees to the direction of whtever foot your using. i.e, a right foot clipper kick would make me turn 45 degrees to the right. Have fun (oooh you will) and keep on kickin'!

You have to be relaxed to do almost any trick. This trick in particular you have to be strong in your leg muscles as with all of freestyle. Practice bending both of your legs and puting your kicking foot behind your support leg and bending down. Make sure the kicking leg extends a good 5 or 6 inches beyond the support leg. This will allow you to make a comfortable stall or kick, whichever you would like to do. Have fun and stay in shape and relaxed, that is the key to great freestyle.

this type of kick is one that, above all else, will open the door to so much cool stuff. The first easy thing to do is do yoga-style muscle building, so that it is easy to simply place and keep your leg back there. Practice. If you get bored with your foot being flat back there, start working on twisting the insole up, so you can do all the quick, effortless grabs that don't rquire the drawn out time a stall takes.

OK this took me a WHILE to get. I've had some trouble with the form and getting the bag to get straight up.

THe Key really is to practice,practice,practice. Ok first start off with setting the bag up. Step forward with your support leg and bend at the knee so you'll have some room for the kicking leg. Put all your weight on the support leg, and lean a little forward for balance. Turn your Torso so your vision range is at the side of you (this is where the bag should fall in range). Watch the bag as it falls down then just bring your leg up behind your support leg(don't FLING it, try to control your kicks). I found that timing is pretty important in this kick. Keep up practicing the form untill you get used to it.

keep practicin up and never quit.
Practice makes perfect

This is a pretty styley kick! Just bend the knee
of your kicking leg and contact the bag when your
calf is horizontal to the ground. I think it
helps control the kick and produce straight sets
if you point your toes and flex your ankle. Your
calf muscle flexes and increases the kicking area
so that it's easier to kick staright up instead
of in a random, uncontrolled direction. Also, you
don't have to snap your leg too hard, just nice
and easy like any other kick. Like the sole kick,
this is done somewhat behind you, so it helps
develop your overall skills if you set the bag
straight in front of you and then turn your body
to do a cloud kick, and turn back to do your next
kick or move. You'll benefit in the long run if
you practice keeping the bag moving straight up
down and turning to do clouds or other "behind the
back" moves.

At first, I wasn't going to post tips for this
move and the flying outside, because I figured
people would figure these out easily on their own.
There is an important thing, though, that
beginners can develop from these moves. That's
"symposium". With other flyers (jester,
butterflier, dragonfly) you jump off of your
kicking leg. You may or may not have known that,
just bear with me... The flying inside can be
performed this way, too. If you set the bag up
and lift your non-kicking leg off the ground, then
jump off your kicking leg and kick the descending
bag with an inside kick, and finally land on your
kicking leg with your non-kicking leg still raised
you have done a really phat flying inside and are
building skills required for symposium moves in
the future. Whew! In short, doing flying insides
by jumping off and landing on your kicking leg
while your other leg is raised is not only really
styley, it's beneficial in the long run. Keep it
up!
Mark is right! Before I looked at it closely, I did not even think of this as a trick. However, I have considered this closely and this is very good practice for the jester and other 'flying' moves
jup with the same foot that your about to kick the foot bag with

hit the footbag high enough to have time for the jump.Jump up with the support leg with the kicking leg at an angle down pointing to the ground.When the footbag descends toward your kicking leg kick it (while in the air)like an inside kick.then land with one leg then the other.Easy as that.Keep trying it till u get it.

If you're doing this move, you might as well
develop some skills you'll need later right?
Well, you can use this move to develop skills
and leg strength required for your future
"symposium" moves! Just stand on your kicking
leg, keeping the other raised, and when the bag
descends to the right height (about waist level),
jump up, do a phat flying outside, and land only
on your kicking leg again. Having your kicking
leg do ALL of the work in this move takes a lot
of strength, but these skills will be helpful in
the future when you're trying your first symposium
mirage! Also, this type of flying outside looks
HUGE and styley. And that's really what it's all
about, right?

I like to use a softer and more loose bag for this...it works better for me, but a thicker, softer, synthetic covered bag that is new could work, but I wouldn't try this trick until you could do other delays like the toe delay, and the inside and outside delays.

-Derrick Towne

There's really not much to say here. Just set
the footbag high so it comes straight down above
you, a few inches above your head. Look up, and
as it descends, bend just a tad at the knees to
cushion the stall. As with every other stall,
you must sink a little to cushion the fall.
That's it! Very styley!

This trick is pretty much self explanitory except for the gentleness part. You have to be able to cushion the fall of the bag and maintain its ballance on your forhead. Kick it high enough to be able to get under it and plan the land. As its decending give way to the bag to cushion it. Be sure to place it right on your forhead. It makes for a pretty styley show and trick. Keep practicing and you'll get it eventually.
This is a lot easier than any of the other stalls for me, because its easier to judge when to dip a little

Not a very difficult trick really. All I can really think of in terms of advice is to hit the footbag so it goes straight above you, then stare straight up as the footbag descends, and bend your knees a bit as it impacts on your forehead to cushion its landing.

Let's face it. The top of your head is really
not the perfect stalling surface for the footbag.
Truthfully, though, it is possible to learn this
trick flawlessly. This one tip allowed me to do
so.
Set the bag a few inches above your head. Get
under the bag and nod, that's right, nod, as the
footbag lands on your head. A little down-up
motion of your head is the way to cushion this
stall. Otherwise, you can't set too high, or
else the bag will bounce off your head no matter
what you do. Only a few inches above the head
will work well. And, look forward, NOT up. Now
you know yet another styley stall!

The major key to this delay is to not set the bag up too high. The higher you kick the bag up the harder it will hit your head and the more difficult it will be to keep it there. makes sense right??? Set the bag up at about your forehead. it will be an easy grab from there.
I found this trick farly easey,just throw the footbag up in the air, once it hits your head slihtly bend down,then let it stay there for 3seconds then you just did a head stall.

heres something fun in lil, beginner hack circles. if u want some inspiration, watch greg nelson, namely his 2003 routine, although its cut off a lil bit, u get the idea for a good one. and watchin greg is pretty cool.

HHey baggers,the only thing you have to do to learn this trick is 1: on your foot find the spot where you wanna stall it 2: get your balance and steady your foot 3: catch and curl the bag on your instep as if it were you trying to stop a puck with your stick in hockey 4: practice for a while and you'll have the flexibility and steadynessto hit it.have fun!

It's all balance. If you can sit "indian style",
then you can do an inside stall. Raise the instep
of your foot to your groin. As the footbag
descends above your foot, lower your stalling foot
a tad slower than the bag's descent to cushion
the stall. The only trouble people have with this
move, is with the balance and with lowering their
foot. Bend a little at your support leg and be
looking straight down at the instep of your
stalling foot to keep balanced.
Ideally, you should be able to lower an inside
stall to the ground and raise it back to groin
level. This will open the door to good, precise
sets, and moves like 'walk over', 'hop over',
'wrap', and 'eclipse'. So while practicing this
move, make sure to focus on keeping your ankle
flexed inward to work those muscles involved.
This stall is pretty important; it will help you
learn the clipper.

Maybe it's just me, or maybe it's just the shoes I wear for playing footbag (my weapon of choice is a pair of closed toe Stone Ridge leather sandals) but I find that if I curl my toes down inside my shoe, it makes it easier to do an inside delay (and a clipper delay for that matter). Partially it gives my sandal a better shape, but more importantly it help me bend my ankle in more so my foot lies flat. But hey, maybe that's just me. If you're having a lot of troubles though, might as well give it a try and see if it helps.

You need to have pretty good flexibility for this trick. Practice just bringing the leg up to the mid section of your body and holding it there. Bending your foot while still in the air. Then your ready for the bag. Drop the bag onto your foot a few times just so you can get the feel of it landing and the easy cution and steadiness you need. Then just do some toe kicks and while the bag is in the air bring the leg up like you practiced and stall it. It's as easy as that, keep practicing and work it till its perfect, i;ll come in handy later.

The best thing to do for this move is to relax, and not get frustrated when it does't work. Just let the footbag hit your foot and instantly bend your support leg's knee. Practice and you will be successful!

Just bend one leg sideways so that your heel is straight in front of your other knee. We used to call it the 'figure 4 stall' because your legs should be in the shape of a 4. Try pickin your leg up like that, And if your leg sorta 'bounces', Just remember to time the bounce so that it just cradles the bag on the way down. And practice.
It seems to me that a person can usually get it if the just bend their opposite leg, while raising their intended delaying foot... and just for fun i used to try and hop over my leg afterwards...

u have to be able to kick the bag in succession with the stallin foot to be able to do this, ive found. just relax, and it should be cake. another thing ive found (and make sure uve got a tough bag if ur gonna try this) is throwin it into a hack circle off of ur inside. right off the ground, put both feet together and tip onto one foot, so u have ur foot sideways on the ground with the bag on top of it, step off it with ur other foot, and throw it in. it shouldnt take u too long to learn

The knee is just another one of those stalling
surfaces that isn't all that great. But it is
possible to master this move once you know how
to effortlessly cushion the stall. Start with
a really, really straight set no more than chest
high. Stick your knee straight out. As the
footbag descends above your knee, cushion the
stall by bending at the support leg. Every stall
involves some sort of sinking movement to cushion
it. In this case, all of the sinking comes from
your support leg; don't drop your stalling knee.
Really styley!

The knee delay can be done slightly differently to produce a very fast and controlled stall. How I do it is to start with the thigh on my stalling leg out parallel to the ground and bent at the knee until my heel nearly touches my thigh. When the footbag is about to touch your knee (just above the patella [knee-cap]) quickly straighten your leg out in front of you like you're kicking another footbag. This causes your knee to lower slightly and to stall the bag. Immediately bring your leg back into a bent position and your knee will raise slightly and release the bag. Pretty neat huh?
For me, when I do the knee delay I lift up the knee and bring my knee down as the hack makes contact. After that it is just a matter of balance and timing.
What you gotta do is get ur right knee straight (if ur stallin it on ur right knee) and then bend ur left knee so you will compensate for the sack hittin ur other knee and it should be coo like that.
The magor key is to lift your hand out and drop the hackeysack when it hits your knee slightly move our knee down.

There's really not much to this trick. It's simple and looks pretty cool. Like any other stall it's all about being smooth and controlled. When you flick the hacky sack up with your foot, try and straighten your knee as much as possible. Also, try and bend your support leg to help cushion the hacky sack when it lands on your stalling knee

I am telling you this move is simple. That's
right, simple. You can set this move to many
different heights and still execute the move. The ideal set
height,( how high you want to kick it)is a little
above your head. DO NOT TUCK YOUR CHIN. This is
the biggest problem I see, person will tuck their
chin and wait for it to fall on their back. Watch
it till you can't. Simple. Keep your head up when
the hack lands on your back/neck, and *cup* it with
your shoulder blades. That means, don't slump
your shoulders, push your shoulder-blades out. The
angle of your back, the raised shoulder blades,
and the -kept-up -head will cup the hacky-sack.
To *launch* it, bow in a fluid manner, snapping
your head at the end of the bow.

This is the easiest move there is. All you have
to do is set the footbag in front of you a little
over your head. The set should be close enough
so that you can just bend at the waist (DON'T
SLOUCH) with your arms out and shoulder blades
sticking up. Keep your head up so that you can
see in front of you instead of at the ground. The
footbag will fall into the cup formed by your
neck and shoulder blades. There are a number of
things you can do to set the footbag to another
move, but the most reliable is to just slide it
off your neck by dropping your shoulders while
straightening out so that the footbag falls
directly in front of you.

All I can really say for this trick is to keep your shoulder blades up and look forward. Once you flick the hacky sack up just position your neck underneath it and it'll stall right where you want it to. Honestly, it's pretty simple and it probably won't take you very long to get the hang of it.

The only difficulty learning this stall really is
just the flexibility of your leg in this position.
The outside of your foot is a great stalling
surface for the footbag: wide and flat. It's
just a matter of your range of motion with your
foot held to the side of your body. Practice,
more than stretching, will help this difficulty.
Just practice outside kicks, close to your body.
That will train those weird muscles involved in
this stall. Focus on bending your support leg
more than lowering your stalling foot to cushion
the stall. On my good side, I can stall at waist
level, lower it to the ground, and raise it back
to waist level. My off side is a different story!
It's just a matter of those muscles! Remember:
practice these close to your body! The balance is
better and the set will be more precise.

When doing this delay, you should concentrate more on flexing your support leg. If you try to hard to recieve the bag with your delay foot, it will most likely just roll off the side. You should also try to delay it just under waist level. Your leg should be mid-way stretched, meaning dont have it fully extended, and dont have it tucked too close to your body. The delay position is a little different for everyone too...just find a position thats comfortable to you. A flat stalling surface is very crucial though. If your foot is at any crude angle, it will be more difficult to pull this off.

This move is really not that hard as long as you remember to move with only your support leg, this will cushion the impact, help keep balance, and help you get it back up just by extending your support leg again.

I found this delay extremely easy and did it the second time I tried. You have to lower your delaying leg like in any delay, but in this one, don't do it by directly lowering your delaying leg. Rather, bend the support leg and let your entire body descend bringing the delaying leg with it. Flexiblity can also come into play if you have difficulty holding your delaying parallel to the ground.

The outside delay is a great stalling surface. Like the other guys have said, it's wide and flat, and it's relatively easy to learn. Pretty much the big thing to remember with this trick is to bend your support leg a bit as you stall it, so your whole body follows the hacky sack down, cushioning the impact on the stalling foot. I actually got the hang of this one faster than the toe stall, so give it a shot. You'll probably surprise yourself

Well, it's a lot easier when you have a hat or cap. Then, just kick the bag in front of your head a little bit over your head level. After that, you only have to move your head a bit foward and the delay should go all by itself. If the bag still falls, just bend your knees. It's a really easy, but really styley trick.
Kick just high enough that you can slide you hat/cap under it and it will be perfect.

This stall looks pretty cool when you pull it off. What you'll want to do is flick the hacky sack up to about your eye level, then duck down a bit so it lands on the brim of your hat. If you want to take this trick up a notch, what you can also do is perform a slight forward flicking motion with your head so that the hacky sack flies off the brim of your hat and onto the top of it. Like all stalls, it's all about control.

i hit this trick the first time i tried it, and i've rarely missed it since. occassionally it will slip due to my head being at a poor angle. set the hack up a couple inches above the beak of your hat, look a little bit upward while landing the stall and it will keep the hack from slipping forward, easy enough. i like to toss my peak stall into a flying clipper. it is super easy to just nod your head off to the side and drop the hack into perfect position for a clipper. it looks pretty slick when you make put it together smoothly.

As with every delay, you have to cushion the
descent of the bag. Set the bag straight up,
about head height, and bend your legs and kind of
lean a little down and to the side to cushion the
bag's fall. Like the description says, you can
use your cheek to catch or grab the bag, but be
careful! For me this was a hard habit to break!
:)

This is something I think is cool but you got to have good flexibility to do it. After the shoulder delay , take your foot and rest it on your knee, then bend over dropping the bag on the sole of your shoe keep in mind that you have to arch your ankle so your sole is bent flat pointing up. then after it lands jump up and hit the bag with the foot you are standing on while you jump.

I don't use my cheek, despite the description. There's nothing to this delay. It's just a matter of kicking the footbag just over shoulder heigth and slightly backwards then turning a tad to let the footbag rest on your shoulder. If you lean back a bit, the footbag will fall in the hollow by your clavicle (shoulderbone), creating a nice pocket to hold it. Flick your shoulder forward continue play.

At first, I started out just throwing the bag up, and then looking back to do a sole kick..but now I just have it down pat, and I can just see the bag go over my head and pull a rippin' sole kick..

-Derrick Towne

Flexibility and a little balance are what's
required to hit this trick. Practice holding the
sole of your foot up behind you. Get the sole
flat and bend your support leg to keep your
balance. If you need to stretch to get your sole
flat, do so, but be careful.
An important thing to remember, though, is that
98% of freestyle moves are set and caught directly
in front of you. The ideal situation is when the
bag just travels straight up and down and the
kicker performs moves around this vertical
movement. So it's better in the long run to set
the bag straight up in front of you, then turn
your body to do the sole kick. Then turn back
after the sole kick to do something else. If you
keep the bag in front of you and turn to do the
sole kick when practicing, instead of kicking the
bag behind you and kicking it back, you may find
that your control of the bag improves drastically!
It'll also be easier to learn new moves this way
because you're learning precise sets, straight and
vertical sole kicks, and how to turn your body!
Good Luck!

Sole Kick,
The main time i have found that i like to use the sole kick is when saving an errand shot while freestylin in a circle of friends. I was once told by a wise man named fozoh (or phozoh or fozo however you wanna say it.) That you might think i have trouble hitting the footbag but fozoh says that if you just look back then youll hit it every time. I tried it and it worked for me so give it a try

What i usually do is start out with the sole kick. it isnt hard as it seems. Kepp your eyes on the ball as it goes up and predict where it'll land by keeping ur soles flat. When you kick it straight up, it goes up. When you kick it on an angle, it'll go that direction, which isnt always good.
the way i do a sole kick is just like an inside, except your ankle is more bent. not a visually impressive trick to one who just watches, but self-satisfying. the behind the back is good for circles

This trick takes speed and flexibility; something that often comes more naturally for some than others, but with practice it can be done and it looks pretty sweet. Bring your kicking foot behind your back, trying to bring the sole of your foot as high and as straight as possible, kicking the footbag in one swift, quick motion using the sole of the foot. Bend your support leg a bit at the knee to help maintain balance.
You can also do this straight out in front of you, pushing the hack to another player...also a good net hack maneuver. Kick straight out, that's about it, just be quick.
Set the bag around head high, or higher. Begin your spin with your head. Spot the bag as soon as you come around.

The spin comes naturally for some, and for
others, like me, it didn't... The spin was
the second to last one-add move I couldn't get
consistent, and now it has turned into my
favorite. Spins are a whole other side of
freestyle and impress newbies sort of like the
pendulum.
Start with the bag stalled on one side of your
body: like on the right side (either a right toe
or left foot clipper). Pretend you will try to
look over your opposite shoulder at the footbag:
in this case over the left shoulder. Now to do
the spin, set the footbag and look over your
opposite shoulder at the same time. (You'll need
to be able to do straight sets, because you won't
even be looking at your set during the spin!)
Allow your shoulders to follow your head around,
and then your lower body will come a split second
afterward. Your head and shoulders will complete
the spin first, so locate the bag now. When your
lower body completes the spin, kick or stall.
The spin involves the coordination of your whole
body; practice channeling the spin from your head
down. Let your shoulders follow your head, and
continue twisting your torso. Don't lift your feet
too far off the ground, just a fraction of an inch
to get your legs around at the end. Concentrate
only on LOCATING THE BAG rather than making
contact, and trust your balance enough to
accomplish this. Your shoulders should be a 1/4th
of the way around when the bag leaves your foot!
Honestly, though, I think spins are much easier
from a clipper stall (less distance to spin!) and
it helps to practice only WITH a bag, not without,
because starting the spin WHILE setting is so
important. Hope this helps other spin-challenged
stylers!

This is a good trick to learn once you've gotten got at the basic kicks. The key here is as you're spinning, turn your head quickly behind your shoulder. This'll help you keep eye contact with the hacky sack as you're spinning around.
How to spin: you may find this site useful. http://www.ballroomdancers.com/Learning_Center/Lesson/2/Default.asp?page=5. If it's dead, search for 'dance spotting'.

The toe delay is the first delay move that I learned, and it's a fundamental building block for the more advanced moves. Before you begin, get yourself a nice soft footbag and a pair of shoes with a wide, flat toe area (Adidas Rod Lavers are perfect). Get in the setup position: stand with your weight balanced on the balls of both feet, knees slightly bent and weight slightly forward. Here's the drill:

1. HOP. To do a right toe delay, drop the bag from about chest height. Just as it begins falling, do a small hop on your left foot at the same time as you lift up your right leg so that your thigh is sticking almost straight out in front of you. Your knee should be bent so that the sole of your foot is parallel with the ground.

2. SINK. Simultaneously squat at your left knee and drop your right foot to catch the bag. Think about cushioning the footbag so that it lands gently on the surface of the foot, just behind your toes. When you catch the bag, your right foot should be just a couple of inches above the ground. Remember to keep your weight forward, it gives you better balance and helps you see the bag as it contacts your foot.

3. SET. Quickly raise your right foot (and straighten your left knee) so that the bag leaves your foot and flies in the air, directly in front of you at about waist height. When doing this, it's critical to remember that your left foot should stay on the ground. You may be tempted to hop on your left foot during the set, but don't.

4. PLANT. Plant your setting foot back on the ground as quickly as possible and transfer your weight onto it.

Before you can claim total mastery of this move, you need to do the right toe - left toe combination. To do this, start with the bag resting on your right toe (right foot just above the ground) and follow steps 3-4-1-2. SET, PLANT, HOP, SINK. Set from the right foot to about waist-level (remember to keep the left foot on the ground), plant the right foot, hop on your right foot as you lift your left leg, and sink to catch the bag on your left toe. Keep repeating this, tossing the footbag from toe to toe. Be sure to keep your weight on the balls of your feet, knees slightly bent, leaning slightly forward.

The only thing I can say is: lower your foot while
stalling!!! Period. When I see people trying to
learn the toe stall, this is the only thing they
have trouble with, every single time.
You must have good balance on one foot with the
other leg held up. As the footbag comes down
over your raised foot, lower your foot slightly
slower than than the bag's descent to cushion the
fall, and end without letting the stalling foot
touch the ground. That's it!
As an aside, the reason people have trouble
with lowering their stalling foot at a controlled
rate is because their balance is a little wobbly.
Just stand there, without a footbag, and stand on
one foot raising and lowering your other foot like
a stall. From here on, every trick you learn
requires balancing properly. As a general rule,
always think about bending your support leg a
little and try to be looking straight down at the
bag as you stall-- for almost every trick. Don't
be discouraged!

In order to stall the footbag on top of the foot,
you have to know this one thing. Bring your foot
down with the bag! This is the basis of a foot stall.
While the hack is in the air and descending, bring
your foot up quickly so it is just below the bag.
Then quickly lower your foot slightly slower than
bag, as to soften the blow of the hack on your
foot. Make sure you are balanced properly on your
support leg so you don't fall over either. Also
important, do not let your stalling foot hit the
ground when it is descending to stall the bag,
this will cause the bag to possibly fall off.
A soft hack is also key in many stalls, so you
should get one if you plan on playing free style.
Hope I helped some of you out there.

This move can be a little scary at first. Just
make sure you have your toe stalls dialed and your
toe sets are very well controlled and vertical.
This move is small and slow, you don't have to
rush it! Just set the bag about knee height, feel
the bag leave your toe and go over the bag with
your thigh. Don't whip your foot out really far.
Your thigh will pass over the bag and once you see
it again put your foot under it and stall. You
should notice that if you do the dexterity with
your thigh like this, your foot never strays more
than a few inches away from the bag. It should
be really easy to get your toe under it in the
end. Finally, it's often overlooked that there
are many different ways to do ATW. Try ATWs to an
inside delay or an outside delay. Try to set ATWs
from insides and outsides. These have been done
from just about every stall to just about every
stall! I saw the Enlightener do a sole set ATW to
a sole stall!!! Practice these types of ATWs when
you're bored or have hit a barrier. Good luck!

You must master this move before you can move on to the 3-add moves. I recommend learning it just after toe delay and inside delay -- these are basically the most fundamental of moves.

Start by reading my description of the inside delay. Basically, a clipper delay is exactly the same, in terms of where you stall it on your foot and relative to the ground. The only difference is that you plant your support leg on the far side of your catching leg as you do the move.

Drill this move by setting it straight up from a right toe delay. Set it the same height as you do for repeating toe delays. As soon as the bag leaves your foot, put your weight onto your right leg, and lift your left leg off the ground just a tad. As soon as the bag starts coming down, keeping your upper body in the same position, jump softly off your right leg, and swing your left leg across to the far side of your right leg, landing on your left leg at the same exact instant as you do an inside delay with your right foot. Since your left leg is crossing to the far side of your right leg, you're doing a cross-body delay.

The clipper delay is a cross body inside delay - this means that the delay foot is behind and on the other side of your support leg.

It is key in most of the more advanced freestyle moves. It took a bit more than a month to start hitting clippers, and a bit more
than a year for them to become easy. Patience is key. Keep trying them over and over - always on BOTH SIDES!

Here are some things to keep in mind:

Curl your toes down into a fist. This makes the inside surface of your foot a bit wider. If you don't believe me,
put your foot in the clipper position and point your toes up? you just lost half of the width of your clipper surface.

Try to get your foot as level as possible. If your toes are pointing toward the ground, the bag will just roll off.
Stretching your ankle helps this.

Bend your support leg (the one you are standing on). By bending your support leg, you can cushion the stall
without having to move your clipper foot as much. This is good because it helps smooth out the stall. The knee of
your support leg should bend just about 90 degrees.

4. You want the clipper to be done beside you, not behind you. If you bend your support leg, there is room for
your stalling leg to go behind your knee and get the foot beside you. If your support leg is straight when you put
your clipper out, it will have to either go through your knee (this is both painful and impossible), or your clipper foot
will end up too far behind you. So, again, bend that support leg.

5. Remember that it is a stall - not a kick. Practice by catching the bag on the clipper. Try to actually stall it -
don't just pop it immediately back up. If you can catch and hold the clipper delay for, oh, let's say 5 seconds, then
you have proven your balance and control.

Remember - USE BOTH SIDES EQUALLY!

Now, for some ways to practice...

Hold the bag in your right hand about waist high. Get your left foot in the cross body position. As you drop the bag,
bend your support leg (in this case, the right leg) and try to GENTLY cushion the bag with your left foot clipper.
You can practice this while you are on the phone, standing in line, or waiting for your corndogs to finish
microwaving. This is the kind of practice that won't make you sweat so you can do it anywhere at any time.
Remember though - USE BOTH SIDES!

Another good way to practice is by catching the bag on an inside delay. Then, wrap your foot around to the cross
body position without letting the bag fall off. This is a lot harder than you think it will be. From this, you will get
better ankle flexibility and a feel for keeping your foot level. This is key for clippers. And, yep, you got it... USE
BOTH SIDES!

And, of course, the most basic way to hit clippers - watch them being hit. In other words, go to a tournament - see
footbag.org for a list of all upcoming events. Or, if you are all alone with no footbaggers around, get Tricks of the
Trade. It is an awesome video with Kenny doing all of the basics. They have slow motion views, a few different
camera angles, advice from the man himself, and demonstrations of good stretches to loosen up those ankles. Every
up and coming player should own this tape. You can buy it from the WFA for about $15. Go to their web page
http://worldfootbag.com and buy the tape. Get some Adidas Rod Lavers and a good facile bag while you
are there. Equipment (flat shoes, soft bag and shorts) will make any move easier

And some drills?

Consecutive Clippers - do as many clippers in a row as you can using the same foot. For timing, I find that it is
important to plant your foot (put it on the ground) between each clipper. Try for twenty or more. As always, you
should go for the same number on both sides.

Alternating clippers - switch feet between clippers. Do a left, then a right, then a left, etc. Some people find it
easier to take steps in between (if you are setting from a right foot clipper, you would first plant the right leg, then
do a little turn and step with your left leg, and then hop forward onto your right leg as you go for the stall on your
left foot clipper). Other people think it is easier not to take the extra steps (from a right foot clipper, set and pivot to
your left, then hop into your left foot clipper). I think that both ways are beneficial and I would encourage everyone
to experiment with both techniques to see which is more comfortable to you.

Alternating consecutive clippers - a combination of the last two. Do a left foot clipper, then a right foot clipper,
then two consecutive left foot clippers, two consecutive right clippers, three left, three right, four left, four right?
See how high you can go. This is a great drill for players of any level.

Once you get clippers, you will be hooked and it will be your turn to spread the word. Clippers are key in 80% of advanced
freestyle moves. If you can do both sides, you can hit many more moves. If you make an effort to do everything that I told you
about, I promise that you will hit clippers pretty regularly within a week or so. Just keep practicing and concentrate on the form.
Think about what both legs are doing - not just the clipper foot. Any other questions, or if I wasn't clear, let me know. Kick on.

Hint:
Delay the bag by bending your support leg more than by lowering the catching leg. That will help you learn.

This helped me learn clipper delays. You will need a videotape (VHS) or a paperback book. Place the book (or video) on your foot in the clipper position. You will probably be falling over at this point, so put your hand on a wall for balance. Now bend your support leg as you lower and raise the book on your foot. Notice how you have to change the position of your ankle to keep your inside surface flat and keep the book from falling off. This will eventually become a very natural and unconscious movement. But in the beginning, really be aware of keeping the inside surface flat.

After a while, remove your hand from the wall. Raise and lower the book on your foot and concentrate on balance. Notice that if your center of gravity, is not directly centered, you will tend to fall over.

One complaint I hear from kickers new to the clipper is "My foot doesn't bend that way in order to maintain the book flat on my foot."
If this is your case, you will have to do a lot of stretching (not all at once, but over a period of weeks/months). Put your foot in the clipper position with the outside of your foot on the ground and push down on the "little toe" or front part of your foot in order to push your heel or back part of the foot down to the ground. You can do this while waiting for the bus, or whenever you are just standing around waiting.

For an even better stretch, do the same as above with the only difference being that you press the front part of your foot on a brick (which is placed on the ground) instead of on the ground. DON'T overdo it, or you will hyperextend. You can train your foot to do the clipper, but it doesn't happen overnight.

Yes, you must learn clippers to become an advanced freestylist, BUT don't let that become your major stumbling block. I have seen more than one talented, budding freestyler quit because he couldn't do clippers. Don't quit beacause of this, you CAN do it. It just takes practice, stretching, and patience.

If you've read any of my other tips, you know I
emphasise balance. In my case, balance has been
my only hurdle in hitting these moves, and in the
case of the clipper, balance is EXTREMELY
important! There is a certain position you must
learn to keep your balance and land this trick.
Otherwise, there's the "magic hop". Both are
required. First the position: stand on one leg
putting all of your weight on the ball of your
foot, NOT your heel. Bend at the knee of your
support leg about 45-90 degrees (you'll have to
lean forward a bit to keep your balance). Flex
the ankle of your free foot inward like an inside
stall. Wrap your free foot around your support
leg to the cross-body position. Now turn your
upper body slightly so you're looking straight
down at the instep of your stalling foot. This
is the ideal position for the clipper; every
clipper should end this way. Difficulties arise
because your muscles may not be used to holding
this position. That's why everyone says to keep
practicing. Your muscles must be trained to
effortlessly assume this position. Now the magic
hop: To understand the hop, try this exaggerated
version. Lift one leg in the air. Hop slightly
off the other leg (leg A) and bring that leg
(leg A)to the cross-body position while landing
on the originally raised leg. Sink a bit into the
landing by bending at the knee. It is the sinking
that cushions much of the stall. Remember: don't
jump (like a jester), just hop. Try the magic
hop and land into the position described above.
The hop both positions you appropriately next to
the footbag AND cushions the stall. Then, if you
land into the position, you will be able to keep
your balance, stall, and make a reliably straight
set. There you go! Keep practicing (I'm sure
you're sick of all of us telling you that)! The
clipper is the BANE of novice footbaggers. This
move takes SO much practice that it weeds out all
of the non-footbaggers from the true footbaggers.
Just don't despair! I hope these tips cut down
your practice time... I wish someone told ME this
stuff!

I've found an easy way to learn a Clipper (or "Jester" as we call it) stall. I'm right footed, so I'll explain it with a right foot.

Perform an inside stall on your right foot
(If you can't do this, you can't Clipper Stall)

Throw it up about waste high

Take two steps in place (right foot, left foot)

As you're coming down on your left foot, bend you knee and hold your right foot near your butt.

When you begin to stiffen your left leg in order to stay standing, start to drop your right foot.

Pending you threw the hack up straightand and your foot is in the right position, you should be able to successfully catch it back on your right foot.

Repeating this process forwards and backwards makes for a nice "hack dance".

A dragon is one of those moves that Jobs' Notation simply fails to conceptualize. You envision it as some kind of crazy contortionist's impossible trick, and for some people, it is ;) Here's what it looks like if you're doing it right. Hold one of your legs out like you're doing an outside delay, and hold it there. Rotate your upper body, and then your support leg. BEND your support leg a LOT. Keep your ankle cranked. When your leg is facing in the other direction, the outside surface will still be facing upward, your toe will be pointing backwards, and viola! That's a dragon position. To do a dragon delay, I recommend setting low from same inside, or you can start on an outside delay and rotate your upper body, and then set from dragon.

This move is the gateway to freestyle footbag. It requires all the elements of the harder moves: a good set, the correct timing, performing a leg dexterity around the bag, the "magic hop", and correct balance and shoulder positioning.

Start with repeating left- and right-toe delays, back and forth (see my hints for toe delay). Set the bag the same height for this trick as for the repeating toe delays.

As soon as the bag leaves your right foot, throw your weight onto your right leg and at exactly the same time you do that, bring your left leg (I mean, *instantly*) to the opposite site of your right leg (i.e., cross your legs without putting your left leg down).

Now, the bag should be just peaking in height, and about to come back down. As it's coming down, swing your left leg (which is now to the right of the bag) *up* and over the bag back to the left (where it started) and switch your weight *onto* your left leg. You can think of this as jumping back off your right leg onto your left leg, while swinging it over the bag as it falls.

The catch is the last part that may take you a while, but keep doing the first steps above until you are comfortable, and don't worry if you are missing the catch. Most people miss it because they either aren't keeping their shoulders over the bag (you *must* hunch slighly forward for this move) so they can't see it, or they don't pick their right leg up in time to do a toe delay. Do both of those, and you will eventually start stalling.

Remember, almost every stall involves the "magic hop" described for toe delays; that means when you jump off your right leg and onto your left leg, at the same time you should be going into the stall motion with your right leg. (In this move, you need to do the hop and stall with the same (right) leg.)

Now, do the left side. Don't master the right before you have tried the left an equal number of times.
Point the toes of the dexterity leg down.

To make this more smooth, jump slightly off the dexterity leg immediately after the set (before the setting leg touches the ground) and begin your dexterity from that jump, then jump off the setteng leg. Also, be sure and keep your body weight forward over the bag.

The mirage is VERY easy once you understand what
to do with your feet. The following exercise
helped me learn this move in one day! Hope it
helps you too!

1) WITHOUT A FOOTBAG: stand on one foot with
your other foot in the air as if you're holding
a toe stall. Now quickly: Pick up the foot you're
standing on while planting your other foot and
quickly do this again so that you're standing on
the original foot. It's just a quick "tap, tap"
or "plant, plant" of your alternating feet. Only
one foot should be touching the ground at a time.
Then, once you understand the rhythm of the
plants involved, throw a dexterity in there!
Again, stand on one foot with the other in the
air. Lift your support leg and throw it around
in a circle from in to out while planting the
other foot. Then, lift the planted foot as your
dexterity is ending and 'viola! You have done
a mirage (though, without a footbag)! It should
- sound exactly the same as without a dexterity:
"tap(dex), tap" or "plant(dex), plant". Only, one
foot touching the ground at a time.

2) WITH A FOOTBAG: Do the above exercise with
a footbag on your raised foot. You must have
complete confidence in your ability to set a
footbag STRAIGHT UP, about waist high. But, don't
get frightened, at first, about the set or the
delay at the end. Just remember the rhythm:
"(set)tap(dex), tap" or "(set)plant(dex), plant".
Your feet begin and end in the same place so
you'll be right back in time to delay the bag.

Lastly, lean your upper body a little foward
while doing the mirage to keep your balance. Look
down at the footbag!

You'll nail the mirage once and wonder why you
ever had any troubles with it before!
Exhilarating! Hang in there!

A lot of advanced freestyle moves end in a mirage. it's a really important move to master. Here's the way I do it -which might not be the best way for everybody.

I set the bag belt-high and try to plant my setting as fast as possible. Once the bag reaches its highest point I just twist my hips so my dexing leg goes under the bag. When the bag is going down I bring my hips back to their original position so the dexing leg goes over and around the bag. Then I toe stall the bitchin' bag!

It's very energy efficient.
Another thing that may help is to try this trick without an ending stall, but instead kicking it while in the air.

It's really important that you concentrate on
the RIGHT thing. I in fact have no problem
with the dex at all (I even master it cleaner
when I set it from the toe), I only fail the mirage when I miss concentrating on the
end stall but on on the dex. In fact, if you
practised a bit, you don't have to give
a da*n about the dex. Learn to throw
that leg over it and believe it'll work.
Instead concentrate only on the stall;
most people start thinking about it when they've done the dex, but that's mostly to late, so try to stall it while you're still dexing it (in your mind for sure)

You don't have to see the footbag to know it's
there. I see a lot of people trying to watch
themselves do the pendulum. This won't cut it.
Once the footbag is on your foot, just begin a
smooth acceleration. (Don't jerk it back) Stay
relaxed. When you look back to see if your doing
it right, you are throwing the balance of your
body, and making it harder to *communicate* with
your muscles and the footbag. Your leg should go
from in front of you to behind you, not to the
side.
*You HAVE to bend your knee and point your toes*

This is one of those moves that if you hit it
once, you'll know exactly how to do it again. The
problem is hitting it that first time, though!
Centrifugal force keeps the bag on your foot.
Start with the bag on your foot (cranked up a
little at the ankle) in front of you and
raised a bit with your leg perfectly straight.
Swing your leg down and back behind you while
keeping your leg straight. At the point where
your leg can't possibly stay straight, bend the
knee and then let the ankle loose and point your
toes. Whew! Got that? Keep the swing in one
smooth motion without speeding up or jerking.
Also, you may need to bend forward a little at the
waist before you release the bag to allow it to
pass over your head. The biggest problem I find
that people seem to have with this move is
balance. When people lose their balance, they
rush the move, lean to much, or try to recover
before the move is complete. If you're having
balance problems, then practice several times
without a footbag. Soon you'll be doing these
without a pause between the stall and pendulum,
without leaning, and with a great release that
falls right in front of you! Remember, this is
a slow move, do it nice and easy, and fluidly!

I found it easiest if you set the bag besides your
body, e.g. from an outside kick or delay. Set the bag
about shoulder high, about 50cm away from your body.
For a right Pincher, lean to the left, lift your right leg,
so that it points a little upward. Try to let the bag fall on to the outside of your knee (the outside should be facing upwards in this position).
In the last moment before the bag hits your knee, squeeze your leg. That's it.

The rake is a truly difficult move to begin learning. The best way of learning this technique is to start learning a pendulum. This is where you catch the bag in a toe stall a little further out in front of you than normal, and then swing your foot backwards past your support leg while still holding the bag. This will get you used to the strange-feeling forces that the bag must go through to not fall off of your foot. To begin a rake, you set the bag a little to the opposite side of the foot you are going to catch the bag with. Then you turn your body towards your catch leg while raising your catch leg into a cross-body like position so you can catch the bag with your toe and drag it across your body. It helps to move your toe fairly quickly once you've caught the bag, just to make sure it can't leave your toe. A good drill is to simply place the bag on your toe and swing it forward and backward over and over to get a good feel for the limits of the move.

Read Rueben Cohns tip first... The best way to think of this trick is an outside stall, that is behind you. The best way to get used to these motions, are doing pendelums, or doing a toe stall and moving your leg in front of you to behind you, to in front of you etc.

Simply be able to lower an inside stall to the
ground without dropping the footbag. To be able
to stand, or put your entire body's weight on the
outside of your foot, is a different story,
though. I know some footbaggers who have weak
ankles that refuse to do this move. Also, if
your ankles aren't flexible enough, be careful
of hyperextension. Lower the inside stall to the
ground, and, like the description says, step over
it with the other foot. To set smoothly and
straight, just bring your foot up EXACTLY like
a clipper kick. That's all there is! Move on to
hop over!

The initial walk over must be quick because almost all of your weight will be pushing down on the top part of your foot. Also stretch those muscles well before hand because pulling those muscles hurt and are a pain to stretch out afterwasrds!

Walkovers are simple but stylish, especially when you can do them quickly. They really work your ankle muscles and builds your flexibility big time. When the stalling foot, try and keep it as straight and steady as possible as you're crossing over with the opposite foot. Flick the hack straight up after this. One thing I like to do is perform a walk-over and then when I flick the hack up is catch it with an inside delay on the foot opposite the one you used before, and try to perform multiple walkovers. By the time I finally learned how to do this move
I'd been able to do the pendulum flawlessly. The
pendulum and wrap are alike in this way: The
carry "feels" exactly the same. If you can't do
the pendulum, this tip might be confusing, because
I will relate the two a bit.
To inside stall the footbag, you should be
lowering your stalling foot to cushion the bag's
fall. (You must have utter confidence in your
inside delays.) Continue to lower your stalling
foot to a level a little above your other ankle.
Swing your stalling foot a LITTLE bit out to the
side and around the ankle of your support leg.
This will add some centripital force to anchor
the bag to your foot. Once around your support
leg bring it up EXACTLY like you're doing a
clipper kick.
Two things may keep you from nailing this trick:

1) You must have PERFECT balance on and over
your support leg.
2) Your wrap must be in one SMOOTH, even motion.
Never speed up, jerk your leg, stutter or
hesitate. The wrap is a slow move. Keep your
balance, bring it down and around your ankle, and
up like a clipper kick to set it straight up.
Confidence, confidence, confidence is all it takes.

The barrage is a fast and fun move that takes you from a clipper set, to a toe stall. First, you don't want to set the bag too high, or it makes it more difficult to learn this move. You want to set the bag from a clipper, then immediately plant your leg (the faster the better), and start to circle the bag with your opposite leg. Raise your thigh until it is nearly 90 degrees to your body and then once you've moved your leg in front of the bag, flick your ankle around the bag again quickly and plant it immediately so you can catch with the other toe. This move feels like only 1 1/2 dexterities because the bag starts on one side of your leg and ends up on the opposite side. Make sure your ankle is not above the bag while doing the dexterities, because that is not really a dexterity and is therefore dubbed "The". Don't ask me why because it's hard to explain.

First, you need to position your foot for an inside delay, but also the delaying surface of your foot should be as far to the side of your body as a clipper delay. I.e. it needs to be in a clipper position, but in front of your support leg instead of behind it. Next, you will jump over your catching leg with your support leg and delay the bag in the air. This is a "power" move, so you should practice jumping over your leg without a bag a few times first to get the feel for it. The catch should be an easy one if you can already do a butterfly because during the jump and the landing your foot will naturally move up and down a bit which should cradle the bag (just like the "magic" hop used to catch every move in a delay), but the key is to make sure that aside from these few inches of vertical movement that your catching leg stays stationary thoughout the jump. Moving it forward with the rest of your body will probably result in a missed catch, while moving it backwards or up will probably launch the bag off your clip instead of having a controlled set. This is the hardest part of eclipse, making sure that the catch is solid so that the set afterwards is useful.

So dont set it higher than your head, the closer to the back of your neck the better. As you watch your set you will note where the bag is going and how fast, so after your duck you'll not only know where it will drop from but where its headed before you get your eyes back on it. Use the Force! Just kidding. I had to drill this move for countless hours before hitting consistently. Here's what I can tell you abou it. It might be good to ask the osis-/torque-master - Steve Goldberg - about this. He helped me a lot. I try to set the bag straight up for this move. If I don't the bag rolls on my foot and falls on the ground more often than not. On a right-foot osis I turn slightly to my left so I can move my foot under the bag. Now my right foot is still on the right side of my body so it's impossible to get a flat surface. I crank my foot as possible but it is still in an almost vertical position. Here is the advice i can offer aboot this move, but i'm no expert because i just now figured it out in my basement. i think i have it down though, and the learning process is fresh in my mind. So here goes (please be sure to tell me right away aboot any misconceptions that you spot):

First, be sure that you already have a high success-rate with your clippers. This is essential, unless you prefer the more frustrating path of backwards-learning.

Start with your foot "cranked" as much as possible to an inside-stall position while your foot is behind you and not yet cross-body. In other words, take an inside stall and rotate it inwards about 90 degrees so that your foot is slightly behind you. *You will not be catching the bag in this position*. This stance is merely for the sake of setting up the move correctly.

Toss the bag straight up on the side where your foot is waiting. Wait until the moment when you would begin to move your foot in order compensate for the cushioning motion of the stall. In other words, wait until just before you start the catching motion.

At this moment, spin into a clipper position *using your support leg*. It is very important that you use your support leg to transform into the clipper position, or else you will end up hurtling the bag away and will likely be unable to stall it this way.

Imagine the whole process as a simple inside stall. Your support leg has the important job of creating the circumstances for this stall. Remember, the stall itself does not occur until the moment you begin to spin and ends in the cross-body position.

The spin itself is not a hopping motion, at least for me. It's more of a pivoting sort of spin with the support leg. Just rotate your support foot as smoothly as possible on the ground. The spin is obviously the most essential part of this move, so it is helpful to practice the motion without the bag beforehand and study the pros a bit first.

Last, as with learning any move, i must stress the following:

As with any move, learn it on both sides at once. *Don't work on one side.* Alternate, or you'll make things much more difficult for yourself in the future.

PS: Ken, i think your problem is that you are trying to catch it before you even begin to spin. Maybe you are not even spinning. Nevertheless, your heel variation could be pretty sweet.

swirl is a pretty simple move, not too big or fast. I recommend that you set is from very low, the outside surface perhaps three inches form the ground. The higher up you hold your dex leg, that is, the closer you bring your leg to your butt, the less mobility you will have availible to you, so if you start low, you will have more room to move your leg to go around the bag. On that note, don't set the bag too high. I ordinarily set the bag to BELOW knee height. the lower you set it, the less you have to raise your leg to get over it, and the less bunched up your leg will get. the set, of course, should be accompanied by a small hop on the support leg. Now I don't even really hop off the ground, just raise up on my toes a bit. The dex doesn't have to go very fast, since your leg doesn't have to move that far, and the slower you do it, the easier it is to stall and really control the bag at the end of the trick. Of course, bend your knee pretty deeply as you stall.
Perhaps the real "trick" to learning a swirl is the learn a Hop-Over Swirl or butterfly swirl first, since these are done faster, and require less precision. You can teach yourself these moves just by jumping over the bag and trying and praying. When these moves start to feel familiar to your legs, you can focus on doing just the swirl without the butterfly, doing it smaller and more precisely. The "Tricks of the Trade" video offers an excellent tutorial on Hop-Over Swirl, which is simply called "the swirl stall" on that video.

Two tricks that really helped me learn Ripwalk were Far Butterflies (clip > same out > op clip) and Mirages (clipper set).

Begin by stringing Far Butterflies together. This will help you in the longer run to do repeated Ripwalks and encourages the use of your flipside.

When doing Mirages from a clipper set, really concentrate on getting your leg over the bag as soon as possible. Try to stand on the ball of your foot, then once you feel the bag leave your clip jump off your support leg. Bend your knee outwards a bit, turning your hips slightly to the outside. Try to let the bag pass underneath your knee and not your calf/foot.

The real trick to Ripwalk is the stepping set, so be sure to learn how to do it both sides. Good luck :)

A torque is a clipper set miraging osis. In other words, you set from a clipper, your other leg does an in-out dexterity, you spin towards the bag and you end the move by catching the bag on your setting foot's clipper.

Key components to a torque are :

the clipper delay

osis

clipper set mirage

Here are some things to keep in mind?

To hit a torque, you have to really focus on making the footbag go under the first leg but not under the second one; the difference is just like the difference between osis and refraction. It's hard to teach osis with words, or torque for that matter, but the key is to put your stalling foot's knee pointing *in* towards your other knee and avoid going over the bag with it. Watch the footbag just as you'd watch it for osis (i.e., try to see yourself stall the bag on your foot before turning your head to the other side for the cross-body pick-up).

There are two types of torques - "crispy" and "original".

I find it easier to do the "crispy" style. This means that you do a blurry set and then bail to osis. If you are hitting blur or stepping butterfly, a crispy torque should be easy for you. For the Blur-set method, you set the bag with a good solid flick up and under your support leg (planting your setting leg immediately as the bag is in the air) to a good height right smack dab in front of you (i.e., belly-button height or slightly lower). From this point, you just do an osis since the bag is already in front of you. There are two deliberate parts to this style of the move.

For the all-in-one "original" method, you set the bag up and slightly across your front, but not too high, and then *wait* for a split second before jumping off your support leg and onto your other leg pointing directly behind you, forcing the spin. And in the middle of all that, in the single jump, your original support leg goes in-out over the bag as your catching foot (knee pointed into other knee) twists out in the osis form to stall the bag. The difference between this and the "crispy" style is that you pause after the set ever so slightly and you do most of the rest of the move in the air. (In a crispy torque, there is a plant and then an osis; two deliberate motions with one leg or the other on the ground almost entirely throughout the move.)

Obviously there are gray areas in the execution of this move, and neither method is better than the other -- it's just how some people conceptualize the move that affects how they hit it. If you're having trouble hitting it one way, you may consider thinking of it the other way. (This is generally true of a lot of moves, by the way.)

Now, for some ways to practice?

Either style of torque essentially involves the same thing - a far osis with a mirage in between clipper contacts. By "far osis", I mean an osis set from the same clipper. This is in contrast to alternating osises which require very little spin between the two contacts. So, to get ready for torque - whether crispy or original - you should practice consecutive far osises. This will force you to spin in one direction and teach you how to keep your balance while spinning. As always, you should practice this on both sides.

If you choose to do the crispy torque, you should really practice your blur sets and your osises. Once both of these components are easy for you, the crispy torque should just fall into place.

To execute the original torque, you should practice clipper set mirages and osises. A bit more of a jump is involved in this style, so perhaps a good cross-training regime of jumping rope or any other good leg exercise would help.

And some drills?

Consecutive Torques - do as many torques as possible on either side. Experiment with the different styles. Try a crispy torque to an original torque. I find it easiest to link crispy torques, but consecutive original torques looks really nice - just watch Peter Irish.

Alternating Torques - since a torque begins and ends on the same clipper, you can't actually alternate them. Try throwing in an osis or an infinity between torques so that you can get both sides. Try torque > infinity > torque > infinity > torque > osis > torque > osis. Keep it going and see how many you can do.
